elements.sent-2411-001: We report a novel, computationally efficient approach for solving hard nonlinear problems of reinforcement learning (RL).
elements.sent-2411-002: Here we combine umbrella sampling, from computational physics/chemistry, with optimal control methods.
elements.sent-2411-003: The approach is realized on the basis of neural networks, with the use of policy gradient.
elements.sent-2411-004: It outperforms, by computational efficiency and implementation universality, all available state-of-the-art algorithms, in application to hard RL problems with sparse reward, state traps and lack of terminal states.
elements.sent-2411-005: The proposed approach uses an ensemble of simultaneously acting agents, with a modified reward which includes the ensemble entropy, yielding an optimal exploration-exploitation balance.
elements.sent-2411-006: Reinforcement learning (RL) is a rapidly developing field of Artificial Intelligence; it allows solving very different problems, ranging from purely engineering, to rather fundamental ones.
elements.sent-2411-007: Numerous efficient RL algorithms have been proposed recently.
elements.sent-2411-008: Roughly, the RL algorithms may be classified as model-free and model-based \citep{dong2020deep , although other classifications are used \citep{wang2019benchmarking .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Among the most popular model-free algorithms are actor-critic class algorithms A2C \citep{mnih2016asynchronous and PPO \citep{schulman2017proximal .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: They demonstrated a great empirical success compared with other RL algorithms \citep{wu2020finite .}
elements.sent-2411-011: A number of model-free algorithms are based on Q-learning \citep{watkins1992q with various modifications, such as e.g. DQN \citep{mnih2013playing , SAC \citep{haarnoja2018soft , and DDPG \citep{lillicrap2015continuous .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: The algorithms like iLQR \citep{li2004iterative , MBMF \citep{nagabandi2018neural , iLQR \citep{li2004iterative and MBMF \citep{nagabandi2018neural are typical examples of model-based algorithms, see \citep{wang2019benchmarking for a review.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Here we refer to another class of algorithms (or algorithms' modifications), which were devised to tackle hard exploration or sparse reward problems, see e.g. RND \citep{burda2018exploration , ICM \citep{pathak2017curiosity , Go-Explore \citep{ecoffet2021first and TR \citep{li2021solving algorithms.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: At the heart of the RL approach is the generation of state-action trajectories (episodes) associated with a particular agent policy.
elements.sent-2411-015: Utilizing the information, gained by the agent in an episode, the policy (that is, an action in response to a state) is improved for the next episode, and the next trajectory is generated.
elements.sent-2411-016: Such an improvement of the policy continues until the best (optimal) policy is achieved\footnote{A number of trajectories may be generated simultaneously, with the policy update based on several successive episodes.. .}
elements.sent-2411-017: In spite of universality of such an approach, which may be deployed for numerous information-processing systems, including robots, advisory dialogue systems (e.g. ChatGPT\footnote{\url{https://openai.com/blog/chatgpt/ ), self-driving cars, etc., for some important for applications problems, RL may lose its efficiency and even fail.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: These problems are associated with (i) a delayed or sparse reward, (ii) lack of terminal states and (iii) state traps; we call them "hard problems" (see the discussion below).
elements.sent-2411-019: All these difficulties stem from the consecutive simulations.
elements.sent-2411-020: To overcome limitations caused by such simulations, one can apply methods of parallel simulations, that is, "whole-states" methods, similar to the Value Iteration (VI) and Policy Iteration (PI) methods of classical dynamic programming \citep{howard1960dynamic .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Still, time discretization of the environment and the application of a discrete mesh of states and actions entail extensive computations for each update step of the action policy.
elements.sent-2411-022: Indeed, such an update is to be done for all available state transitions.
elements.sent-2411-023: This can be extremely demanding, in terms of memory and CPU time, especially for high-dimensional state space -- the so-called "curse of dimensionalily"\citep{friedman1997bias,bengio2005curse .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Another class of RL approaches developed to tackle hard problems refer to the Demonstration Learning, which includes techniques like Behavior Cloning (BC) \cite{bain1995framework , Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) \cite{arora2021survey , Offline Reinforcement Learning (Offline RL) \cite{levine2020offline , and new approaches like Diffusion Policy \cite{chi2024diffusionpolicyvisuomotorpolicy and Reverse Forward Curriculum Learning (RFCL) \cite{tao2024reverseforwardcurriculumlearning .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Here an expert demonstration allows an agent to learn a satisfactory policy relatively fast, even in challenging environments with sparse and delayed rewards, high-dimensional action spaces, and state traps.
elements.sent-2411-026: Unfortunately, the ability to incorporate expert knowledge directly into the learning process is not always possible; moreover, such methods do not allow to obtain policy with a maximal reward.
elements.sent-2411-027: Next, one should mention Exploration Bonus methods -- the RL strategy aimed to encourage agents to explore less familiar parts of the state space.
elements.sent-2411-028: By providing additional intrinsic rewards, this methods enable agents to discover states and actions, missed due to sparse rewards.
elements.sent-2411-029: They include: Curiosity-Driven Exploration \cite{pathak2017curiosity , Random Network Distillation \cite{burda2018exploration , Count-Based Exploration \cite{bellemare2016 and Policy Entropy Maximization \cite{mnih2016asynchronous and also suffer from significant limitations.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: In particular, these methods heavily rely on the intrinsic rewards, which may not always align with the tasks associated with external goals.
elements.sent-2411-031: Furthermore, there is no universal recipe of construction intrinsic rewards -- it has to be done ad hoc for each problem.
elements.sent-2411-032: Similar techniques is the Reward Shaping, which may be exemplified by Intermediary Rewards for Low-Level Goals \cite{dorigo1998 , Potential-Based Reward Shaping \cite{ng1999 and Learning-Based Reward Shaping\cite{jaderberg2017 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Here the reward is modified in order to provide more informative feedback to the agent.
elements.sent-2411-034: It is particularly valuable in environments where rewards are sparse, delayed, or difficult to obtain.
elements.sent-2411-035: The reward reshaping guides an agent toward the desired behavior and helps to accelerate learning.
elements.sent-2411-036: Nevertheless, although this technique can be highly effective, it possesses notable limitations.
elements.sent-2411-037: That is, designing an effective shaping rewards requires at least approximate knowledge of an optimal policy.
elements.sent-2411-038: A lack of such a knowledge would result in a poorly designed shaping functions yielding, not optimal policy and/or unreasonably long learning time.
elements.sent-2411-039: Exploration-first methods, such as unsupervised exploration \cite{Laskin2020,hazan2019provably strategies and systematic exploration methods like Go-Explore \cite{ecoffet2021first , have been designed to address hard problems, which also contained a requirement for complex, long-term planning.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Still, even an exhaustive exploration (without optimized exploration-exploitation balance) cannot guarantee that the optimal policy, with a maximal total reward will be found.
elements.sent-2411-041: Finally, one can mention another class of RL methods which address hard problems -- Intermediate goal-based approaches, such as Hindsight Experience Replay \cite{andrychowicz2017 and Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning \cite{nachum2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-042: These methods introduce sub-goals or intermediate objectives that guide the agent's learning process, making it easier to reach the final goal.
elements.sent-2411-043: The deficiency of these methods stems from their high computational complexity and the need of designing appropriate intermediate goals; these may be specific for particular problem.
elements.sent-2411-044: Hence, this approach is not universal.
elements.sent-2411-045: Here we propose a new universal approach for solving hard problems of RL, which overcomes limitations of the consecutive simulations.
elements.sent-2411-046: It is computationally efficient and free from the "curse of dimensionality".
elements.sent-2411-047: Instead of dealing with a single agent or a finite number of agents, as in conventional RL, it deals with a continuous ensemble of agents (see a more formal definition below).
elements.sent-2411-048: The agents are characterized by a set of (state) variables, varying in some intervals.
elements.sent-2411-049: Such an ensemble is described by a distribution function for these variables, effectively corresponding to an infinite number of agents.\footnote{Note, that application of ensembles provides an additional flexibility of an approach, therefore ensembles are effectively used in various settings, e.g. for the uncertainty estimation for Bayesian models \citep{lakshminarayanan2017simple , transition probabilities evaluation \citep{dimakopoulou2018coordinated , etc. Conceptually, our approach is similar to Umbrella Sampling of classical Monte Carlo, utilized in Computational Physics and Chemistry.}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Hence the term "Umbrella RL" is used.

elements.sent-2411-055: We also compare the performance of Umbrella RL with other, relevant for hard problems, RL algorithms and demonstrate a significant supremacy of the new approach.. The final Sec.

elements.sent-2411-057: There are three main features of hard problems, where RL fails or dramatically loses its efficiency:.
elements.sent-2411-058: A sparse, or significantly delayed reward; e.g. the reward may be gained only at the final state -- the goal.
elements.sent-2411-059: A presence of state traps, where an agent gets stuck, which are difficult to avoid.
elements.sent-2411-060: A lack of a certain terminal state, e.g. the final goal may correspond to multiple states or a state region.
elements.sent-2411-061: The most prominent examples of hard problems are depicted in Fig. \ref{fig:hard_prob .}
elements.sent-2411-062: The Mountain Car problem \citep{moore1990efficient , Fig. \ref{fig:hard_prob a, illustrates the first difficulty.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Namely, the agent here is a car, randomly placed in a valley.
elements.sent-2411-064: It may accelerate in either direction, which corresponds to possible actions, but cannot overcome the downhill force due to the gravity.
elements.sent-2411-065: The goal is to reach the top of the hill.
elements.sent-2411-066: This may not be generally achieved by simple motion in one direction, but only by swinging in the well with an increasing amplitude, applying an appropriate acceleration in appropriate time instants.
elements.sent-2411-067: The top of the hill is the final position, and only there a reward is given.
elements.sent-2411-068: In other words, any intermediate reward is lacking.
elements.sent-2411-069: This complicates an application of RL which guides, by rewarding, towards a better (and eventually to the best) policy.
elements.sent-2411-070: A simple random strategy -- a random acceleration here, generally fails.
elements.sent-2411-071: Similarly, in the Multi-Valley Mountain Car problem, the agent is rewarded only reaching a zone between flags, see Fig. \ref{fig:hard_prob c.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Here the agent needs not only reach the zone with the reward, but also balance within the zone, without any terminal state.
elements.sent-2411-073: Another hard RL problem is the Acrobot problem \citep{sutton1995generalization , Fig. \ref{fig:hard_prob b, where an "acrobat" needs to swing appropriately, to lift up to the target height, where the reward is given.}}
elements.sent-2411-074: The same occurs in the StandUp problem, illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:hard_prob d -- the reward may be received at the very end of a successful action, that is, in the target region.}
elements.sent-2411-075: A safe evacuation from a hazardous place, when a leader (e.g. a specialized robot) needs to guide a crowd towards an exit, e.g. \citep{bahamid2020intelligent , belongs to the same class of problems.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Saving lives is the main goal there; hence, a reward is given only when all humans are evacuated.
elements.sent-2411-078: Again, there is no way to assess an efficiency of a strategy until the final state is reached.
elements.sent-2411-079: The list of examples may be continued.
elements.sent-2411-080: The second difficulty, of getting stuck in local state traps, is generic.
elements.sent-2411-081: Generally, it is associated with properties of the state-action space.
elements.sent-2411-082: For instance, when an agent gets stuck in a minimum of potential energy (like in Mountain Car), or when optimization of a policy drives it toward a local maximum of the expected return.
elements.sent-2411-083: State traps cause a failure in walking of a humanoid robot -- here "get up to go" requires much more complex dynamics than simple walking straight \citep{kuindersma2016optimization .}
elements.sent-2411-084: Finally, the third difficulty, is related to the lack of a terminal state.
elements.sent-2411-085: It is also generic and arises in numerous applications.
elements.sent-2411-086: For instance, when learning certain movements, e.g. learning of an agent to walk \citep{haarnoja2018learning , or learning a defensive behavior by a swarm \citep{nuzhin2021animals , a particular terminal state may be hard to define.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Indeed, how is it possible to judge, whether an agent has already achieved an efficient enough strategy? Such a conclusion seems to be very subjective.
elements.sent-2411-088: In other words, it is hard to define a terminal state in advance, that is, a clear criterion to cease simulations is lacking.
elements.sent-2411-089: Noteworthy, the above difficulties do not simply sum up, but enhance each other, making hard problems with a few difficulties even more challenging.
elements.sent-2411-090: Fortunately, the novel approach, reported here, resolves all the above problems.
elements.section-2411-003: The main ideas of Umbrella RL
elements.sent-2411-091: All the above hard problems difficulties stem from the consecutive nature of simulations, associated with a generation of state-action trajectories for a singe agent, or a limited number of agents.
elements.sent-2411-092: What happens if one deals with a continuous ensemble of agents, instead of discrete agents? By a continuous we mean an ensemble where the agents are characterized by a set of state variables of probabilistic nature, with the magnitudes varying in certain intervals.
elements.sent-2411-093: It is described by a distribution function $p(s,t)$, where $s$ is a multidimensional variable, specifying agents' states and $t$ is time.
elements.sent-2411-094: Correspondingly, $p(s,t)ds$ -- quantifies the number of agents with the state variable $s$ belonging to the small interval $(s,s+ds)$.
elements.sent-2411-095: The intention to use an ensemble is motivated by a somewhat similar problem in computational physics and chemistry, where Monte Carlo (MC) method is used to compute thermodynamic properties.
elements.sent-2411-096: In this case a direct application of Metropolis-Hastings algorithm may result in a stuck of a Markovian chain, associated with generated molecular configurations, in a local potential minimum.
elements.sent-2411-097: This prevents exploration of the whole configurational space.
elements.sent-2411-098: The Umbrella Sampling MC provides an elegant solution to this problem: The Markovian chain is associated now, not with a single thermodynamic state, but with a whole ensemble of different thermodynamic states, covering a certain region of thermodynamic phase space, as an umbrella \citep{Umbr,FrenkelBook .}
elements.sent-2411-099: Hence, even if some states (cold and dense) act as a trap for a Markovian configurational chain, the presence of other states of the ensemble (hot and rarefied) drive the Markovian chain out of the trap, preventing getting stuck.
elements.sent-2411-100: This allows a successful exploration of systems with complicated potentials and multi-phase systems, see e.g. \citep{Valleauinbook,Valleau2005,BV1998,BV1998a .}
elements.sent-2411-101: Here we adopt the main idea of Umbrella Sampling to RL, that is, we propose an approach where an ensemble of agents explores simultaneously a whole range of states and actions.
elements.sent-2411-102: Hence the "umbrella" refers to the state-action space and the update of the policy is performed with the data accumulated from multiple realizations of the environment.
elements.sent-2411-103: For the same reasons as for Umbrella MC in Computation Science, such an algorithm will avoid the state traps.
elements.sent-2411-104: Moreover, Umbrella RL, additionally, resolves two other difficulties of hard problems; this is demonstrated in the rest of the article.
elements.sent-2411-105: To this end, we need to design a method, where an ensemble of agents evolves in time, along with the evolution (towards the optimal) of the action policy.
elements.sent-2411-106: It should evolve in a way enabling the most efficient exploration of the environment.
elements.sent-2411-107: This would minimize time, needed to reach the desired states with a reward and help to avoid trapping.
elements.sent-2411-108: Yet, the evolution of the ensemble should dramatically change when such states are reached.
elements.sent-2411-109: In terms of the exploration-exploitation trade off, we aim to construct an ensemble, that tends to be the most extended one, as long as states with a reward are lacking.
elements.sent-2411-110: Simultaneously, it should be the most condensed, once such states are reached -- condensed around the states with a reward.
elements.sent-2411-111: As we show below, this may be achieved, combining the conventional reward and entropy of the ensemble.
elements.sent-2411-112: Note, that the entropy of ensemble is joint state-action entropy and differs from the policy entropy \citep{ahmed2019understanding generally used for regularization in RL.}
elements.sent-2411-113: The policy gradient method has demonstrated its high efficiency and is incorporated in many RL approaches.
elements.sent-2411-114: Here we generalise this method, to deal with a continuous ensemble of agents.
elements.sent-2411-115: It is also convenient to consider continuous time.
elements.sent-2411-116: We apply the conventional assumption, that the system follows Markov decision process \citep{guo2009continuous,agarwal2019reinforcement , so that agents make decisions (about actions) in accordance with a random policy \(\pi\).}
elements.sent-2411-117: The actions $a$ depends on the state only, that is, \begin{equation}\label{1} a\sim \pi(a|s).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-118: In other words, the action \(a\) is defined by the action policy \(\pi = \pi(a|s)\).

elements.sent-2411-121: The aim of RL, is to obtain an optimal policy \(\pi^*\), that is, the policy maximizing the total reward of the agent, which always acts in accordance with the policy \(\pi\).
elements.sent-2411-122: For Markovian process it is determined by $r(s,a)$ -- the reward the agent receives for an action $a$, made in a state $s$.
elements.sent-2411-123: The overall quality of a policy is quantified by the expected return, $J(\pi)$ -- the weighted sum of all rewards received on the state-action trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-124: For Umbrella RL and continuous time it reads, \begin{equation}\label{3} J(\pi) = \int_0^\infty \gamma^t \left< \E_{a_t \sim \pi} \left[r(s_t,a_t) \right] \right>_{p_t}dt,\end{equation} where $p_t$ abbreviates the distribution $p(s,t)$, depending on the policy $\pi$ and $\left< \ldots \right>_{p_t $ denotes the averaging over the distribution $p(s,t)$.}

elements.sent-2411-130: Obviously, such a policy optimization, for the expected return, Eq. \eqref{3 , would hardly work for hard problems, where a reward is given only in a small fraction of final states.}
elements.sent-2411-131: Therefore, the search for an optimal policy should be based on a generalized expected return, that contains a term, stimulating exploration of states.
elements.sent-2411-132: This term should dominate in the lack of states with reward, but be negligible, when such states appear.
elements.sent-2411-133: Based on this, the following generalized expected return is proposed: \begin{equation}\label{5} {\cal J}^{\rm URL}(\pi,p_t)=J(\pi) + \alpha \, H[p_t \cdot \pi],\end{equation} where $J(\pi)$ is given by \eqref{3 and \(H[p_t \cdot \pi]\) is the joint entropy of the ensemble state distribution and action policy, \begin{equation}\label{5a} H[p_t\cdot \pi ] = -\int_0^{\infty} \gamma^t\left< \E_{a_t \sim \pi} \left[\log (p_t \cdot \pi)\right] \right>_{p_t} dt\end{equation} and \(\alpha\) is the entropy weight.}
elements.sent-2411-134: Noteworthy, $H[p_t\cdot \pi ] $ is similar to the conventional entropy based on the trajectories [see e.g. \citep{TrEnt ], but with the time discount factor $\gamma$ and probability \(p_t\) under the logarithm.}

elements.sent-2411-180: To illustrate a practical application of the general ideas of the Umbrella RL, we analyze a few hard RL problems.

elements.sent-2411-182: In the Multi-Valley version of the Mountain Car problem, a central (highest) hill separates a number of hills and valleys to the left and to the right from the central hill, see Fig. \ref{fig:hard_prob c.}
elements.sent-2411-183: The agent receives the reward only in a narrow range of positions (between two flags) on the top of the central hill.

elements.sent-2411-189: The problem is formulated in terms of two state variables specifying the agent (car) position $x$ and its velocity $\dot{x}$.
elements.sent-2411-190: The environment is determined by the current height $y(x)$, \begin{equation}\label{height} y(x) \!= \! \frac{1}{10} \! \left[\cos \left(2 \pi x\right)\! + \! 2 \cos \left(4 \pi x\right) \!- \!\log \left(1 - x^2\right)\right],\end{equation} which depends on the car position $x$.

elements.sent-2411-287: We propose a novel algorithm for Reinforcement Learning (RL) to solve hard problems.
elements.sent-2411-288: These problems, in contrast to regular ones, are associated with the following complications: (i) a significantly delayed reward, given at final states only, (ii) the existence of state traps, that are difficult to avoid and (iii) the lack of a certain terminal state.
elements.sent-2411-289: We demonstrate that the new algorithm being universal in implementation significantly outperforms, the state-of-the-art RL methods, most of which fail in solving hard problems.
elements.sent-2411-290: The computational superiority of the new approach will further increase with the increasing complexity of the system.
elements.sent-2411-291: The main idea of the method is to apply a continuous (i.e., effectively infinite) ensemble of acting agents, instead of a limited number, as in conventional RL.
elements.sent-2411-292: As the result, the modified expected return, quantifying the efficacy of the action policy, is estimated for the complete ensemble of states and actions.
elements.sent-2411-293: It comprises the conventional expected return of RL, as well as the term, specifying the ensemble entropy.
elements.sent-2411-294: To find the optimal policy we apply the appropriate modification of the policy gradient method.
elements.sent-2411-295: The policy optimization in the modified approach is entangled with the optimization of the distribution function specifying the agents' states.
elements.sent-2411-296: In practical implementation of the method, neural networks are exploited, which makes the algorithm very efficient.
elements.sent-2411-297: We demonstrate the application of Umbrella RL to the Mountain Car problem in its multi-valley version and to the StandUp problem.
elements.sent-2411-298: Here we address the most simple models to illustrate the main ideas of Umbrella RL for hard problems, with minimum details.
elements.sent-2411-299: Certainly, the new method may be applied for more practical cases, e.g. for "Robotic arm" problem -- our StandUp problem is the according "minimalistic" model.
elements.sent-2411-300: The Multi-Valley problem, the more complicated version of the original (single-valley), may be considered as a 'minimalistic' model of a self-driving car; it possesses all the three difficulties of the hard problems.
elements.sent-2411-301: The performance of the Umbrella RL (UR) has been compared for these models with other state-of-the-art RL methods, which are expected to be the most efficient for hard problems.
elements.sent-2411-302: Among these are the proximal policy optimization (PPO), which is believed to be the most effective and universal RL method, the RND method, that is a prominent method for hard exploration problems and iLQR algorithm, which is another popular model-based RL algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-303: Additionally, we apply the value iteration (VI) algorithm, which guarantees the convergence to the global optimum.
elements.sent-2411-304: We demonstrate that the new Umbrella RL significantly outperforms all the above algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-305: Moreover, most of these algorithms (PPO, RND, iLQR) fail to solve the Multi-Valley Mountain Car and StandUP problem in general case, that is, for arbitrary gravity strength.
elements.sent-2411-306: Although VI could solve these problems, it demonstrated significantly lower efficiency for sufficiently small time-step, associated with large gravity, furthermore, it required significantly larger memory.
elements.sent-2411-307: To illustrate the universality of Umbrella RL, we present here its continuous-time version.
elements.sent-2411-308: Naturally, it may be also applied for any problem with discrete time.
elements.sent-2411-309: The discrete-time version may be formulated in a very similar way.
elements.sent-2411-310: This is important, since the most of RL problems are formulated in discrete time settings.
elements.sent-2411-311: The discrete-time modification is based on the same ideas.
elements.sent-2411-312: Namely, one needs to assess the distribution of agents in the environment, which stems from the action-policy distribution.
elements.sent-2411-313: Then the ensemble of agents is to be used to improve the action policy.
elements.sent-2411-314: Simultaneously, it should be used to control the exploration-exploitation balance of the policy.
elements.sent-2411-315: Usually, discrete time simulations are computationally simpler.
elements.sent-2411-316: In the present study we report one of the possible versions of the Umbrella RL --- the model-based version.
elements.sent-2411-317: Its generalization, for a model-free version may be also possible and will be presented elsewhere.

elements.subsection-2411-009: Derivation of the evolution equation for $p(s, t)$
elements.sent-2411-319: The derivation of the equation for $p(s,t)$ is straightforward, as it is essentially a continuity equation.
elements.sent-2411-320: Let $p(s,t)$ be the state distribution for an ensemble of agents at time $t$.
elements.sent-2411-321: Then $p(s,t) ds$, gives the probability that an agent resides at time $t$ in a close vicinity of the (multi-component) point $s$ of the state space, where $ds$ is the respective volume of an infinitesimal element, centered at the point $s$.
elements.sent-2411-322: Consider a closed domain $\Omega$ in the state space with the boundary $\Gamma$, and with the vector $e(s)$ being a unit external normal to $\Gamma$ at the point $s$.
elements.sent-2411-323: The probability current through the surface element $dS_s$, located at the point $s$ of the boundary $\Gamma$, may be written as $p(s,t) (v\cdot e) dS_s $, where $v=v(a,s)=ds/dt$ is the vector of local probability current.
elements.sent-2411-324: It depends on both -- the state $s$ and the action $a$, determined by the policy $\pi(a|s)$.
elements.sent-2411-325: The total local probability current equals the sum of all currents corresponding to different actions $a$, that is, to $p(s,t) \left< v\cdot e \right>_{\pi dS_s $, where $\left< \ldots \right>_{\pi}$ denotes the averaging over the policy $\pi$.}
elements.sent-2411-326: Hence, the rate of change of the total probability to reside within the domain $\Omega$ reads, \begin{equation}\frac{d}{dt} \int_{\Omega} p(s,t) ds = -\int_{\Gamma} p(s,t) \left< v\cdot e \right>_{\pi} dS_s = - \int_{\Omega} \nabla_s \left[ p(s,t) \left< v(a,s)\right>_{\pi} \right]ds,\end{equation} where Gauss theorem has been applied.
elements.formula-2411-016: \begin{equation}\frac{d}{dt} \int_{\Omega} p(s,t) ds = -\int_{\Gamma} p(s,t) \left< v\cdot e \right>_{\pi} dS_s = - \int_{\Omega} \nabla_s \left[ p(s,t) \left< v(a,s)\right>_{\pi} \right]ds,\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-400: The boundary conditions are a part of the environment.
elements.sent-2411-401: They are applied to the probability distribution and value function and define, what are the properties of the value function and agent distributions for the observation space boundary.
elements.sent-2411-402: In the present work, we embed directly the boundary conditions in the neural network.
elements.sent-2411-403: Let \(NN(x)\) be a neural network with a scalar output and input vector \(x\); we impose von Neumann boundary conditions: \begin{eqnarray}\frac{dNN(x)}{dn}\bigg|_{\Gamma} = 0,\end{eqnarray} where \(\Gamma\) is the boundary and \(n\) denotes the extrnal normal to the boundary \(\Gamma\).
elements.sent-2411-404: In the present work, we combine the NN of two parts such as: \begin{equation}NN(x) = NN_h(h(x)),\end{equation} where \(h\) is environment specific representation function and \(NN_h\) is a neural network taking state representation \(h\) as an input.
elements.sent-2411-405: The goal of the representation function is to guarantee the boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-406: If we impose the representation \(h(x)\) as, \begin{equation}\frac{d h(x)}{dx} \cdot n \bigg|_{\Gamma} = 0,\end{equation} then \begin{equation}\frac{dNN(x)}{dn}\bigg|_{\Gamma} = \frac{dNN_h(h)}{dh}\frac{dh(x)}{dx} \cdot n \bigg|_{\Gamma} = 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-407: That is, So, manipulating the state representation, we can impose arbitrary(including the reflecting) boundary conditions.
elements.subsubsection-2411-002: Boundary conditions for Multi-Valley Mountain Car
elements.sent-2411-408: Reflective representation \(h\) was applied to impose the probability distribution and value function to satisfy boundary conditions (it is not required for the action policy).
elements.sent-2411-409: As a hidden state, we generate a three-dimensional vector h, such as: \begin{equation}h = \begin{pmatrix} x \hat{v}^2 d_{min} \cdot \hat{v} \end{pmatrix},\end{equation} where, \begin{equation}\hat{v} = \cos\left(\frac{v-v_{min}}{v_{max} - v_{min}} \pi\right)\end{equation} is normalized velocity, and \begin{equation}d_{min} = \min\big(|x-x_{min}|,|x-x_{max}|\big)\end{equation} is a distance to the boundary.
elements.sent-2411-410: Boundary values of the agent speed are \(v_{min/max = \pm 0.07\) and boundary positions are \(x_{min/max = \pm 0.99\).}}
elements.sent-2411-411: The hidden representation was developed, such that the boundary conditions \begin{eqnarray}\frac{dh}{dv}\bigg|_{v = v_{min}/v_{max}} = 0\end{eqnarray} and \begin{eqnarray}h(x,v)\bigg|_{x = x_{min}/x_{max}} = h(x,-v)\bigg|_{x = x_{min}/x_{max}}\end{eqnarray} were satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-412: This results in the corresponding reflection boundary conditions for the agents probability distribution \(\overline{p}\) and the value function \(V\): \begin{eqnarray}\frac{dNN(x,v)}{dv}\bigg|_{v = v_{min}/v_{max}} = 0\end{eqnarray} and \begin{eqnarray}NN(x,v)\bigg|_{x = x_{min}/x_{max}} = NN(x,-v)\bigg|_{x = x_{min}/x_{max}}.\end{eqnarray}.
elements.sent-2411-413: The boundary conditions representation for StandUp problem is defined for environment outputs \(\phi_1 \in (0,\pi)\) and \(\phi_2 \in (-\pi,\pi)\).
elements.sent-2411-414: The state representation was developed to reflect approximately the environment dynamic on the state boundary, which is required for a proper work of the UR algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-415: The state representation translates the space with a complex shape, which has, specifically, a restriction of the form \(\phi_2 \in (-2\phi_1 , 2 \pi - 2\phi_1)\), to a simple square domain \( \theta_1,\overline{\theta _2 \in (-\pi/2,\pi/2)\).}
elements.sent-2411-416: The new space allows assigning of reflective boundary conditions with a simple \(\sin\) function.
elements.sent-2411-417: That is, To be precise, the state representation reads, \begin{equation}h = \begin{pmatrix} \sin \theta_1 \sin \overline{\theta}_2 \end{pmatrix},\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\theta_1 = \phi_1 - \frac{\pi}{2},\end{equation} \begin{equation}\overline{\theta}_2 = \frac{1}{2}\frac{\theta_2}{1 - |\theta_1 / \pi|},\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\theta_2 = \phi_1 + \phi_2 - \frac{\pi}{2}\end{equation} One can show that such state representation guarantees, that: \begin{equation}\frac{dNN}{d \theta_1} \bigg|_{\theta_1 = \pm \pi/2} = 0\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\frac{dNN}{d \overline{\theta}_2} \bigg|_{\overline{\theta}_2 = \pm \pi/2} = 0.\end{equation}.
